pages.title: Welcome to Government Systems & Targeted Attacks
pages.subtitle: Learn why local governments are attractive cyber targets and how employees can help reduce risk.

video.caption: Introduction to targeted attacks against government systems

pages.title: Why local governments are targeted
pages.text:
Local governments may not seem like obvious cyber targets, but they often manage essential public services and sensitive operational data, including public safety systems, water or utility operations, payroll and finance systems, court or administrative records, permits and citizen services, and internal communications.

Attackers know that even a small government office may feel pressure to restore services quickly. That makes local agencies attractive targets for ransomware, account compromise, payment fraud, and other disruptive attacks.

A cyber incident in local government can affect real services that communities depend on every day.

pages.title: Not every attacker wants the same thing
pages.text:
Government agencies can face different types of cyber threats.

  • Some attackers want money through ransomware, extortion, or fraud.
  • Others want access to systems, sensitive information, or internal communications.
  • Some groups are patient and highly targeted and may stay hidden while collecting information or waiting for the right time to cause disruption.

You may hear these more organized groups described as Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs). Employees do not need to memorize that term, but it helps to understand that some attacks are planned, patient, and highly targeted.

image.alt: Different government cyber threat actors (replace later)

pages.title: Did you know?
pages.text: Even smaller agencies can be attractive targets when they support important public operations.

bullets.title: Small agencies can still be high-value targets
bullets.text: Attackers do not only go after major cities or federal agencies. Smaller local governments may have fewer security resources and still run important services.

bullets.title: Disruption can damage public trust
bullets.text: When systems go down, residents may lose access to services and confidence in the agency's ability to operate safely.

bullets.title: Social engineering is still one of the biggest risks
bullets.text: Attackers often try to manipulate employees before using technical methods.

bullets.title: Early reporting can stop bigger problems
bullets.text: Strange login alerts, unusual messages, missing files, or unexpected system behavior should be reported quickly.

image.alt: Government cybersecurity awareness facts graphic (replace later)

pages.title: Why this matters to everyday staff
pages.text:
Cybersecurity is not only about servers and technical tools. Government employees can become targets through everyday work. Attackers may impersonate vendors, supervisors, agency partners, law enforcement, state officials, or IT support. They may use email, phone calls, text messages, fake login pages, or urgent requests to manipulate employees into sharing information or taking unsafe action.

A single mistake can lead to account compromise, data exposure, service disruption, or a larger incident affecting the community.

Everyday employees can play an important role in stopping targeted attacks early.

pages.title: Common warning signs
pages.text:
Some attacks begin with subtle signs before major damage is obvious.

Watch for things like:

  • Unexpected password reset or MFA notifications
  • Suspicious messages pretending to be from leadership, vendors, or IT
  • Files, folders, or shared drives behaving strangely
  • Login alerts from unfamiliar devices or locations
  • Requests for sensitive information that feel urgent or unusual
  • Systems suddenly slowing down, failing, or locking users out

These signs do not always prove an attack, but they should never be ignored.

pages.title: Real-world government impact
pages.text:
A recent Ohio example shows how a cyberattack can quickly affect a local government. In late 2025, the Village of Golf Manor dealt with a ransomware incident that disrupted systems and raised questions about whether officials should pay to restore access.

Reporting on the case said the village considered a $10,000 ransom demand, and later coverage noted that council approved payment of $2,000. The case also drew attention because Ohio law now places restrictions on when a political subdivision may comply with a ransom demand.

Under Ohio Revised Code Section 9.64, a political subdivision experiencing a ransomware incident shall not pay or otherwise comply unless its legislative authority formally approves the payment or compliance in a resolution or ordinance explaining why it is in the political subdivision's best interest. The same law also requires reporting the incident to the executive director of Ohio Homeland Security as soon as possible, but no later than seven days after discovery.

When government systems are disrupted, the whole community can feel the impact.

Reference video: YouTube source.

References: DataBreaches coverage | Ohio Revised Code 9.64

pages.title: Targeted threats and national impact
pages.text:
As mentioned earlier, some advanced targeted attacks are not just about money. Some attackers may want long-term access, disruption, or sensitive information.

This reference video is older, but it helps show how serious targeted attacks against government systems can be.

If you think you may be dealing with an advanced targeted attack, notify the federal government as soon as possible. Lives, public safety, and critical services may depend on it.

Reference video: YouTube source.

Federal reporting resources: IC3 | CISA Report an Incident | FBI Tips

video.caption: Reference video on targeted government cyber threats

pages.title: What employees should do
pages.text:
Preparation matters before an incident happens.

Local governments and municipal utilities can reduce risk by taking practical steps ahead of time:

  1. Keep systems updated. Regularly patch operating systems, software, and device firmware, especially for internet-facing systems. Remove end-of-life devices and address known vulnerabilities.
  2. Plan for manual operations. Critical public services such as water treatment, energy delivery, and emergency communications should have continuity plans if computer systems or automation become unavailable.
  3. Isolate critical infrastructure. Separate operational technology and utility control systems from internet-connected business networks whenever possible.
  4. Limit remote access. Remote access should be tightly controlled and protected with strong multi-factor authentication.
  5. Be cautious with fear-based sales tactics. Security decisions should be based on actual risk and operational need, not pressure or urgency from outside vendors.

If you notice suspicious activity or receive an unusual request:

  1. Pause before acting.
  2. Do not share passwords, MFA codes, or sensitive data.
  3. Verify unusual requests through trusted channels.
  4. Follow your agency's incident response or reporting plan if one exists.
  5. Report suspicious activity immediately using your agency's approved reporting process.
  6. Write down or record useful details such as the time, sender, phone number, message content, affected system, screenshots, or unusual behavior, if it is safe to do so.
  7. Contact the appropriate outside resources when needed.

Helpful reporting links:

  • Ohio Cyber Integration Center (OCIC): CyberOhio OCIC page.
  • Ohio reporting guidance: Ohio Homeland Security reporting guidance.
  • Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3): www.ic3.gov.
  • FBI Cincinnati: FBI Cincinnati Field Office.
  • FBI tips: tips.fbi.gov.

Preparation resource: League of Minnesota Cities – Global Cybersecurity Conflicts.

Ohio contact details: OCIC@dps.ohio.gov | 614-387-1089.

Fast reporting helps the agency detect, respond, and recover before a problem grows worse.
